message.content:
You are an independent blind verifier (verifier A) for the hc-assay hardening campaign at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-10). Decide TRUE_POSITIVE (genuine production-readiness defect to fix) or FALSE_POSITIVE (false/already-mitigated/out-of-scope-by-design/not real). Out-of-scope: dataset-agnostic opaque inputs; optional import-guarded backends; intentionally-committed scrubbed transcripts (but a NEW leaked PII/secret class IS in scope); on-box data-sovereignty; documented honest-scope. Read real code and REPRODUCE where testable (cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python ...; "python -m pytest"; gh). Judge ONLY P10-CI-1.

FINDING P10-CI-1 (high): require_green_ci.sh ignores headSha → stale-green run certifies an untested commit
Location: scripts/require_green_ci.sh
Claim: The pass-9 pre-merge gate runs 'gh run list --branch <b> --workflow ci.yml --limit 1' and passes if that newest run is completed/success — it never compares the run's headSha to the branch HEAD (or PR merge SHA). So (a) in the window after pushing a breaking commit B but before its run registers, the gate returns A's old green run and B merges un-gated; (b) the documented 'require_green_ci.sh && gh pr merge' decouples the inspected branch from the merged SHA. This is the SOLE merge gate (branch protection is 403). VERIFY: read the script; confirm headSha is not compared; confirm gh run list returns newest-by-createdAt regardless of commit.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this finding by reading the script and testing the claims.

content.content:
1	#!/usr/bin/env bash
2	# Pre-merge CI gate (#CI-1) — compensating control for an unenforceable required status check.
3	#
4	# Branch protection / required status checks are UNAVAILABLE on this repo's plan: it is private on
5	# a free tier, so `gh api repos/<owner>/<repo>/branches/main/protection` returns HTTP 403
6	# ("Upgrade to GitHub Pro or make this repository public"). GitHub therefore does NOT block merging
7	# a PR whose CI is red — the `all-checks` fan-in job is computed but nothing consumes it as a gate.
8	# That is the exact mechanism by which CI went silently red across several hardening passes (merged
9	# via --admin / un-gated). Making the repo public to enable protection is out of scope (it would
10	# publish the committed transcripts); upgrading the plan is an operator/billing decision.
11	#
12	# Until one of those changes, THIS is the gate: it exits non-zero unless the latest `ci` run for the
13	# branch concluded success. Run it before every merge and refuse to merge on failure:
14	#
15	#   scripts/require_green_ci.sh [branch]      # defaults to the current branch
16	#   scripts/require_green_ci.sh && gh pr merge <n> --merge --delete-branch
17	set -euo pipefail
18	
19	branch="${1:-$(git rev-parse --abbrev-ref HEAD)}"
20	read -r status conclusion < <(
21	  gh run list --branch "$branch" --workflow ci.yml --limit 1 \
22	    --json status,conclusion -q '.[0].status + " " + (.[0].conclusion // "none")'
23	)
24	echo "latest ci run for '$branch': status=$status conclusion=$conclusion"
25	if [ "$status" = "completed" ] && [ "$conclusion" = "success" ]; then
26	  echo "CI is green — safe to merge."
27	  exit 0
28	fi
29	echo "::error::CI for '$branch' is not green (status=$status conclusion=$conclusion) — do NOT merge (#CI-1)." >&2
30	exit 1
31

content.text: The finding is confirmed by reading the script. Let me verify the `gh run list` ordering claim.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && gh run list --branch harden/pass-10 --workflow ci.yml --limit 5 --json status,conclusion,headSha,createdAt 2>&1 | head -40
input.description: Inspect recent CI runs with headSha and createdAt

content.text: No runs for this branch yet, but let me confirm `gh run list` ordering semantics and check whether the script's documented usage truly decouples inspection from merge SHA.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && gh run list --workflow ci.yml --limit 5 --json status,conclusion,headSha,createdAt,headBranch 2>&1 | head -40
input.description: List recent CI runs across branches to confirm ordering

content.text:
Confirmed. `gh run list` returns runs newest-by-createdAt, each carrying a `headSha`. The script discards `headSha` entirely.

The finding is fully verified:

1. **Script reads only `status` and `conclusion`** (lines 20-23): the `--json status,conclusion` projection never requests `headSha`, and the gate condition (line 25) compares only `status="completed"` and `conclusion="success"`. The branch HEAD SHA is never computed or compared.

2. **`gh run list --limit 1` is newest-by-createdAt** (confirmed empirically — runs come back ordered by createdAt descending), so the gate trusts whatever the most-recently-created run happens to be, regardless of which commit it tested.

3. **Both exploit windows are real:**
   - (a) After pushing breaking commit B, before B's CI run registers, `--limit 1` returns A's older green run → gate passes → B merges un-gated. This is a genuine TOCTOU race, not theoretical.
   - (b) The documented usage `scripts/require_green_ci.sh && gh pr merge <n> --merge` (line 16) inspects the branch by name but merges by PR number; nothing pins the green run's SHA to the SHA actually merged. A push between gate and merge slips through.

4. **This is the sole merge gate** — the header (lines 4-13) documents that branch protection returns 403 on this plan and `all-checks` is computed but unconsumed, so there is no defense-in-depth behind this script.

A correct gate would resolve the branch HEAD (`git rev-parse origin/<branch>` or the PR's merge/head SHA) and require a *completed success run whose `headSha` matches*, failing closed if no run exists for that exact SHA. The script does none of this.

The 403 branch-protection limitation itself is documented honest-scope, but the defect here is that the compensating control is itself broken — it can certify an untested commit. That is a genuine production-readiness defect in scope.

input.reasoning:
Verified by reading /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/require_green_ci.sh and reproducing gh run list semantics.

(1) The script's gh call (lines 20-23) projects only `--json status,conclusion` and the gate (line 25) checks only status=completed && conclusion=success. It never requests or compares headSha against the branch HEAD or PR merge SHA. Confirmed headSha is not compared.

(2) Reproduced `gh run list --workflow ci.yml --limit 1`: runs return newest-by-createdAt descending, each carrying a headSha that the script discards. So `--limit 1` yields whatever run was created most recently, independent of which commit it actually tested.

(3) Both exploit paths are real: (a) TOCTOU window — after pushing breaking commit B but before B's run registers, --limit 1 returns the prior green run for commit A, so the gate passes and B merges un-gated; (b) the documented usage on line 16 (`require_green_ci.sh && gh pr merge <n>`) inspects the branch by name while merging by PR number, decoupling the inspected/green SHA from the merged SHA.

(4) This is the SOLE merge gate: the header (lines 4-13) documents that branch protection returns HTTP 403 on this private/free-tier repo and that the computed all-checks fan-in is not consumed as a gate. There is no defense-in-depth behind this script.

The 403 plan limitation is documented honest-scope, but the defect is that the compensating control itself can certify an untested commit. A correct gate must pin the green run to the exact branch-HEAD/PR SHA (e.g. resolve git rev-parse origin/<branch> and require a completed-success run with matching headSha, failing closed when none exists). Genuine, in-scope, fixable production-readiness defect.

content.text: Verified P10-CI-1 as TRUE_POSITIVE. The merge gate at `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/require_green_ci.sh` checks only the newest CI run's status/conclusion and never compares its `headSha` to the branch HEAD or PR merge SHA, so a stale-green run can certify an untested commit (both the push-before-run-registers race and the inspect-by-branch/merge-by-PR decoupling). It is the sole merge gate since branch protection is 403 on this plan.
